speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Kurt Saunders on August 16th, 1996. What and who made the decision that you and your brother would leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, I-- I was the one that pushed that we should get out of Austria, that there isn't any future in Austria, because I believed what I read. I believed in Hitler's

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, I was 19 years old. I wasn't 19 yet when Hitler marched in, but I was 19 in June of the same year. And I went to Vienna several times to see whether we could get a visa to one of the countries. And I realized-- I came back and told my parents it's impossible. Nobody wants the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number-- a number of a visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had nothing. And the-- even though I said we're going to get affidavits and I know there will be work done in America, nobody wanted Jews. There were endless lines in front of every embassy in Vienna and there wasn't an opening to go anywhere. There just wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where-- what-- when did you make that decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came back and I says there isn't. But I have heard that there's such a thing as leaving Austria illegally and go to another country. And I sat down with a map and I says, here, the German side, there are all these borders. The only way I feel we could escape is through Germany, because nobody is going to stop us there, going to Germany. And went back to Vienna and I found people that had set up call it railroad, whatever it was, where you left Vienna and you went to Germany. You went to Cologne, Germany, and from Cologne, to Aachen. And in Aachen, you had three borders there. So if you couldn't get into one country, you could get into another one. At that time, I only had-- one side you had Luxembourg and France. And the other one, you had Belgium, then you had Holland. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you got out of Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: How we got of Vienna? Well, I came home when I had [INAUDIBLE] I forgot already how much it. It was several hundred shillings. Austrian currency is shillings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they called smugglers or were they called-- what was the German name or the Austrian name for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no name for-- they were just people helping us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: -- who were helping other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: --other Jews escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But along the road was Gentiles, were Christians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had-- who had to be paid off?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. The guy in Vienna got the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right? And everything was arranged.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What'd you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home. It was 11 o'clock at night. And I said, I'm leaving tomorrow morning at 2 o'clock, on next train out. And I'm taking my brother along. Now, that was quite difficult, because my brother was my mother's darling. But we showed them, showed them the plan. I says to them, if everything works out fine, you will follow us. And we left 2 o'clock in the morning. We were up all night talking, talking, convincing they should let us go. Got to Vienna. In Vienna, we took the train to Germany and arrived in Cologne in the evening. And the very funny-- funny. It wasn't that funny to us. We were scared, really scared. We wound up the hotel called the Bayerische Hof. The Nazis had the convention there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bayerische Hof.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Barisha Hof?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Bavarian Court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. 'Bavarisha Hof.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Bayerische. Bayerische means Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I know. Hof. OK. Hof. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Hof. H-O-F. And he got there and I said, my god, we can't stay here. So my brother and I decide we're going to go to a movie and come back midnight, when it's dark. Go to the movie and they were showing a newsreel of the Reichstag-- not the Reichstag, I mean in Nuremberg the Nazis had their big conventions and Hitler made that speech and everybody hollered heil. Their arms were up. And people were laughing. They were laughing in the movie theater. And we've just come back from Austria. I said to my brother, Walter, the lights are going to turn on. All of us are going to be arrested. Nothing happened. This was the Rhineland. And the people in the Rhineland near the French border, they're much more liberal. They were laughing. To them it was a joke. Well, we went back to the hotel. And the next morning, we took the train from Cologne to Aachen, Aix-la-Chapelle, the French call it. And there we were supposed to get into the cathedral and [INAUDIBLE] other than remain in the cathedral, and then somebody's going to pick us up after dark. And that's what happened. We got to the cathedral. Actually, somebody informed us-- German informed us that there were-- there was police at the final station, Aachen, that there's a chance might get arrested. So we got off the stop before and we walked into Aachen. Got to the church and we were picked up by a gentleman, his name was Kaufmann. He was Jewish. His wife was Gentile, was a Christian. She had a corset shop right in the center of town. We got into the corset shop. They kept us there 'til after dark, real dark. She closed the store. And the next-- it was on a Monday. The next two days we spent at their home. And it was explained to us how we're going to get across the border, that first we have to go into regular border patrol. They have a hut there. They'll check us out. And then we go into-- there's a promenade, they call it. It's actually a walk to the town of Bleiberg in Belgium. And in Bleiberg, Belgium, they have-- every Wednesdat they have German women go there on the pilgrimage. They pray to the statue of Mary there. And these are women that can't conceive. And that statue is supposed to help them to conceive. That we're going to join that group, that pilgrimage in the forest there on the way to Bleiberg. And the night before, Mr. Kaufmann took us to a gasthaus, which is like a bar and grill. We sat down and then he asked my brother Walter, who was only 16 years old, go over and get me a glass of beer. Walter went over, got him a glass of beer. Then he asked me to get him a glass of beer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, he didn't drink the beers. I asked him how come. He said, you see these people in uniform over there. They are at the border. They're the border patrol. I want them to know that you guys are coming across tomorrow. Yeah? So the next day, we got to the border. They permit us to take 10 marks out, which is $2.50. No, it's $4. I'm sorry. Those days it was $4.

speaker: interviewee
text: Each of us, we had to strip. They searched us. Then they ask us what we're going to do in Belgium. We said, we're going to Belgium. Hopefully, we'll be going to America. And the German told us right then and there, don't stay there too long, because we'll be in Belgium soon. That was 1938. They knew already what was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you when you left Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I wore a raincoat on top of my jacket. I had a sports jacket on and a pair of pants. Wore a raincoat. In that raincoat, I had a shaving brush, because I shaved twice a day. I had a shaving brush and a shaving cream, a toothbrush, toothpaste. And my brother didn't shave yet. He had something-- not-- a couple of handkerchief, a change pair of socks, that's it. Whatever we wore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Money.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Some money. Yes, we had some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mementos of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- this Mr. Kaufmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet him? In Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time in Aachen. He came into the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Aachen.

speaker: interviewee
text: They picked this up at the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So basically, from Vienna to Aachen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Aachen. We were alone on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we looked at the beautiful Rhine. And we looked at the Rhineland and the vineyards and so on. The ride-- the train ride was beautiful. The only thing, honestly we were scared that somebody might think or realize we're Jews, you know. We always felt that being a Jew is written on your face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any narrow escapes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, crossing the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went across the border, these were the days of Munich. It looked like there's going to be a war. Belgium Army was at the border. Chamberlain was in Munich, negotiating with Hitler. And this was the first day of Rosh Hashanah, 1938, September, and we were crossing the border. When we got past the German border and we were supposed to meet that pilgrimage-- and this was about 11 o'clock, we were told we were-- had to cross the border in the morning, to wait for them till about 11 o'clock. And we were hiding there. We were not standing a road. We were hiding. And came 11 o'clock, 11:30, quarter to 12:00, no pilgrimage inside. So my brother and I got out. And we had been told not to leave the road, because if you go down the hill there, the Belgium troops already-- they says, we might be arrested. This is the Maginot Line. And we stopped some people, Germans, that were promenading. And we asked them if there's any patrols ahead. And they said, yes, on the-- actually on the road, that you have to identify yourself and show your papers. And well, we decided to go anyway. By the way, the Kaufmann's gave us a sandwich along. We had a sandwich in a pocket, each of us. And we did go down the road. And by the time we got to the bottom of that hill, it was a little clearing. And there were Belgian troops there having lunch. It was lunchtime. And they were busy talking in French, naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and my brother said, what are we going to do? There are soldiers here. I said, get out your sandwich and eat too. So that's what we did. And we passed them and we got by them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we hit the main road. We got to a main road. And lo and behold, there was the pilgrimage, the main road. And we joined them. And we had been told ahead of time that if they stop us, if-- that we should-- when-- if we are in the back, we're going to join it, that we should advance through the center to the front as they check the carte d'identite, identification cards. Everybody in the world had an identification card. And sure enough, this happened. This was a Catholic procession. They carried the statues. The boys were swinging incense and so on. And they came. They stop it. And it worked. Nobody stopped us. As we pretend we were praying first. Then we got to the church, again the church in Bleiberg. And all the procession, everybody walked into the church. And I remember like today, I put my hand in the holy water. There was a priest standing there. And I put it in deep and my hand came out dripping. And I looked at the priest and said, by god, he knows I'm not a Christian. He knows I'm a Jew. Didn't stop us. We got into the pew. When they knelt down, we knelt down. Then after dark a cute little blonde came in, Gentile, Christian girl. And she knew, because we were the only two left in the church. They took us to their hotel-- they had a small hotel-- into the kitchen. They fed us. And we sat there 'til about 11 o'clock at night. Then she told us she's going to take us across the fields. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a contact person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name, by any chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She's going to take us across the fields. And I don't remember what town she said. That's where we going to meet the bus. It's going to be a couple of hours we're going to walk during the night across the fields and they're going to take us to a bus. And she's going to get the tickets for us, put us on the bus. She's going to be with us on the bus 'til the next town. We're going to get the railroad tickets and send us to Brussel. in Brussel, we have a contact. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: So time came. We were walking. Naturally, it felt like it's endless at night, in strange country. We got to the bus stop. There were big searchlights at the bus stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bus pulled up about 5 minutes, 10 minutes later. And she said to-- spoke to the driver. And the driver said he had three checkups already. He doesn't think there'd be any more checkups. Got on the bus. We walked all the way to the back. That's where she wanted us to sit. And about 10 minutes later, big searchlights again. The whole place is bright light. And soldiers are coming on the bus. Madame, monsieur, [FRENCH]. They're asking for it. And we're sitting back there. We don't have it. She says, take your passport. She said to us, fold it inside out so that the picture is showing. And that's what you do. And keep your fingers crossed. They came to the back, the soldiers, with the flashlight, pointed at, looked and said merci. That was it. And maybe it was lucky that these were the days of Chamberlain and war years. If regular patrol, border patrol, would have known this was not identification, it was a German passport. I guess the soldiers didn't know any better.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the bus continued. We got on the train. And from there we left for Brussel. In Brussel we had an address. There were some other refugees there. Rue Bodeghem 56. I remember the number. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rue Bodeghem 56. And we got a taxi. And it took us there. It was 2 o'clock in the morning. And we called up and they were on the third floor and we rang the doorbell. Fellas came down we had never met before. They took us upstairs. They had a small room. They had a very large bed. There were four of us going to sleep in the same bed. And next morning was the second day of Rosh Hashanah. And we were told the committee is closed because of the holiday, that we should go down to the kitchen where they feed the refugees, see the rector, and you tell them that you're just arrived and they'll feed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish home, or a--

speaker: interviewee
text: The home? No. The home, the apartment was rented above a bakery, and unique place. The baker himself had supported in the last election Degrelle, who was the Belgian Nazi. And his bakery was the headquarters for-- this was a Jewish section.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baker?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the Nazi--

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Degrelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-E-G-R-E-L-L-E, Degrelle. And that-- Degrelle, and that's a Belgian Nazi. He was the leader of the Belgian Nazis. And this baker was supporting him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were housed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who rented the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose house was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It belonged to the baker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Did he know you were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had all the other Jewish refugees in the building?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he being paid for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Degrelle lost the election. And that baker had lost his Jewish customers. Now he had to do something to get them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was scared stiffless. He was so scared it was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were moments, yes. There were moments.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you most scared of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they're going to deport us back to Germany. And also we went down to the kitchen. It was a very unique experience. We went in there and we asked, we just arrived. We asked him to feed us. And he looked at us and said, when did you arrive here, the director, in Yiddish. He spoke Yiddish to us. I said, today, this morning. You traveled on the second day of Rosh Hashanah. I have no food for goyims. And he did not feed us. He refused to feed us. He had no food for goyims.

speaker: interviewer
text: This whole trip, were you prepared in advance for what-- you seemed to be able to go step by Step Were you prepared for it? Did it go the way it was supposed to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was prepared to do something. I'm 19 years old in those days. You would think different than you think at my age now. You-- I'm an optimist. And we could handle it no matter what.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, if I'm deported back to Germany, I had no intentions of going back to Germany, because they drop you off at the no man's land. And I would sit in the no man's land 'til after dark just to turn around and come back. And today you read Mexicans are doing a very similar thing, right? And I had no intentions of going back. No matter what they do I was going to stay. What happened after you settled in? Did you remain in that with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not too long. We-- what actually happened is between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur-- when I crossed the border, I must have hurt myself somewhere. I had a scratch on my left arm and I got blood poisoning. And even my glands under my left arm were swollen. And normally you would have gone to the Jewish committee to see a Jewish doctor there. They take care of you. But again, we're in this period of Munich and the committee was closed. And Belgium was actually preparing for war. That's what it looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wound up in the Catholic hospital. And from Yom Kippur, I spent the night in the hospital. They cleaned it up. They put me on I don't know what medication. I don't think they had antibiotics in those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, I got out of the hospital on Yom Kippur and had another experience. Well, walking out, I always observed Yom Kippur. I always fasted. I didn't have any food. And I walked by the temple and I heard the prayers and I walked inside. And there was usher standing there, right up there near the hospital. I asked them whether I could sit down. I told them I just came out of the hospital and I escaped from Austria and Germany. And he said, get-- go upstairs and get a ticket.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked out. I wasn't used to have to get tickets. I had? I found out different. In America, you got those tickets. I guess in Belgium you had to have a ticket too. So I walked out. I did fast. But I didn't get into any temple on Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Jewish people later on, became friendly with. And we used to-- when they started rounding up refugees, we used to get up early in the morning, go to the forest if the weather was nice, and I spent the day in the forest. I come back at night. And I always told my brother, if we go to sleep, never leave the key when you lock the door on the inside, because if somebody looks through the keyhole, you see a key, they know you have to be inside. You have to be. If they knock on the door at any time, we just don't answer. And if it wasn't-- if it was raining, we would go to a movie. In those days, they had movies that's played continuously. I think had in the United States too. You could go from one, you could sit there for four hours to watch movies. Or we would hide in the bakery. He used to keep his bags of flours on top of his ovens. We would hide behind the flour bags. Actually, it was more convenient to go to the movies. It was cheap too. It was only a couple of francs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- where did you get money to live on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish committee gave us 42 francs a week, which was very little, if you realized that $1 was worth 50 francs. But we got one meal. Half of it went for rent. We-- we got a room in that same house. And the other thing is there were days you got hungry, so we developed a way of stilling our hunger. We would take a piece of bread and go in front of a delicatessen store, break the bread in half and get the whiff coming out, so when you had a bag of bread, the other bread was the hot dog or the hamburger or whatever it was, a piece of corned beef, whatever smelled good, a chicken, a roast chicken. So this is how we used to fill our stomach, take a bite of bread. And occasionally we spent some money on ice cream. They had delicious ice cream-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any relationships with anybody in-- you were in Brussels--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with people in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: You asked me before whether we brought any clothing across.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, I think it was, had a distant cousin, a diamond dealer in Brussel. And we got his name and address. And they sent us two suitcases, one for my brother, one for me, to this cousin, distant cousin. I remember like the day we went up there and he says, you're going to America? They don't speak any Yiddish. You're going to be lost. He had been to America before. [LAUGHS] They don't talk Yiddish. He says, you're going to be lost. Anyway, we got the suitcases, so we had some thing, a few things.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- you were able to do what with these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a change. We had a suit to wear. We had something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about money other than the 42 francs?

speaker: interviewee
text: They mailed us some money, not too much, because there were limitations how much money you could send out, right? And we got some money, not a lot. I did a little business. There was such a thing to get around those controls, I would buy international stamps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And actually no, it was this way. My parents would buy international stamps and I would sell the stamps there, turn them into cash, and then send back a form of money, I forgot already what the deal was. But each time we made an exchange-- between Austria and the stamps an exchange-- I had some dollars. Well, not dollars, had some francs to spend. Did

speaker: interviewer
text: You work there at all, other that--

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not let you work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked earlier whether you made any friends amongst the people that were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of people were you with? Where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're talking in camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in-- in the-- not the camp, but in that house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I was arrested?

speaker: interviewer
text: --over the bakery. Who were the people you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were from Vienna too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I made friends with Belgian Jews. They even took us out to see a Jewish play. As a matter of fact, it was the Jewish play that we went to. And then they took us to restaurant for dinner, that we didn't get home 'til very late.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, I said to my brother, never leave the key? I left the key in the door. And 4 o'clock in the morning, there was a knock on the door and the police was here. So we were arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked and said, oh my god. I says, the key's in the door. I didn't say it in English. I said it in German. [LAUGHS] And I said, oh. This is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Open up the door and there's three policemen or two policemen standing. And they said, come with us. I said, do I need anything? No, don't worry. You'll be back right away, which was a lie.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sent to prison. We spent a week in prison. And during that week, we were handed a questionnaire to ask us whether we're political refugees. And naturally, Jews were not recognized as political refugees. I was in a single cell. My brother was in a single cell. And once a day they would take us out. We would march in circle in a courtyard with other prisoners, Belgium refugees, Belgian people. And I remember even like today they would holler at us, [FRENCH], and tell us we couldn't talk. And they called my name. And I-- at that time, I made up my mind to whether I'm going to change my name, because they couldn't pronounce my name. They would say, 'Monsieur Scotts-care, Scotts-care. And it came out very funny the way they said it. I said, well, I got to change the name, because nobody can pronounce this German name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, when we handed those questionnaires, I started thinking, how can I become a political refugee? And well, I-- I've been lucky all my life. I had an uncle, my father's youngest brother, even though my grandfather was a rabbi, was a Communist from way back.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had graduated from the University of Vienna as a lawyer, international law, one of the youngest graduates. At the age of 21, he graduated with a law degree. And during the height of the Depression, he spoke several languages. And he learned Russian in a hurry. There was an opening for a editor for the international paper. And he became the foreign editor-- editor of the in Moscow. And after five years being in Russia, he came back and became the editor of the Rotes Wein, which was the Daily Worker, the Communist paper in Vienna. And he also defended some Communists in court.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I realized that I put down that this uncle of mine, the Communist, is endangering us too, because we have to go back to Germany, the Germans, the Nazis in particular, don't recognize that you could be innocent, guilty by relationship, guilty by association. And we all know that this is possible. It has happened here too, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in America, it wasn't like it was in Germany. That meant concentration camps. So I put down that for that reason we request that we could stay in Belgium. And it worked. A week-- within the week, we were let go. But both of us, my brother and I, get a piece of paper that said that within five days we have to leave Belgium proper. So they didn't deport us, but they gave us what they called a further route. Get out. And the League of Nation agreement that was signed, if you're a political refugee, you're only political refugee, asylum has to be given to you only in the first country that you enter. If I leave Belgium and I go to France or Holland, it's not. Then I'm just a stateless person that can be deported to any other country or back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to change our tape and find out what happened within the five days.
